title: Psalms 28
subtitle: The LORD Is My Strength

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “Do Not Be Deaf to My Cry”

heb.paragraph: "To you, LORD, I call; you are my rock (ṣūrî), do not turn a deaf ear to me" (v.1). David cries to God as rock — solid, immovable, reliable. Without God’s response, David is like those going down to the pit (v.1). He lifts his hands toward God’s inner sanctuary (v.2) and asks not to be dragged away with the wicked (vv.3–4).

refs.note: "The LORD is my rock" — the same ṣūr imagery.
The LORD as 'my rock' — the same geological metaphor for divine protection and stability that opens Psalm 28's appeal to the God who is both refuge and strength.
The LORD as rock, fortress, and deliverer — an expanded version of the same fortress theology that Psalm 28 develops from individual plea to royal intercession for the people.

notes.note: "To you I call, my rock. Hear my cry for mercy as I lift my hands toward your Most Holy Place." Prayer directed physically toward the temple — embodied worship.
"Because they have no regard for the deeds of the LORD, he will tear them down and never build them up." Ignoring God’s work leads to permanent destruction.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'my Rock' (ṣûrî) — the metaphor dominates: a rock is silent, immovable, unyielding. The psalmist fears that God's rock-like silence could become the silence of death (the pit). He needs the Rock to speak.

Alter: 'who speak cordially with their neighbors but harbor malice in their hearts' — the gap between speech and intention defines the wicked. Alter notes the social specificity: these are neighbours, not strangers.

Calvin: 'if you remain silent, I will be like those who go down to the pit' — Calvin reads divine silence as the worst judgment. Not punishment but absence. God's speech is life; God's silence is death.

Calvin: the imprecation ('repay them for their deeds') is not personal vengeance but an appeal to divine justice. Calvin distinguishes between desiring revenge (sinful) and desiring that God uphold moral order (righteous).

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'the pit' (bôr) refers to the grave or the netherworld — the realm of the dead. To descend to the pit is to be cut off from God's presence and from the worshipping community.
NET: 'toward your Most Holy Place' — debîr qodšekā, the inner sanctuary (Holy of Holies). The psalmist lifts hands not generally skyward but toward the specific place where God's presence dwells.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: 'do not turn a deaf ear' — literally 'do not be deaf to me.' The pun on Rock is intentional: a rock is naturally deaf. The psalmist begs the Rock not to act like one.
Kidner: the wicked's fundamental error is not moral but perceptual — they 'have no regard for the deeds of the Lord.' They do not see God acting in the world. Blindness precedes wickedness.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the prayer's urgency ('do not be silent') reveals the covenant relationship's dialogical nature. Israel's God is a speaking God; his silence would contradict his revealed character.

VanGemeren: 'the works of the Lord' — failure to perceive God's activity in creation and history is the root of wickedness. The remedy is not mere moral effort but restored perception of divine action.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the address 'my Rock' sets up the psalm's central tension. A rock provides stability and protection but is also cold, silent, inanimate. The psalmist needs the Rock to be warm and responsive.

Goldingay: 'speak cordially but harbor malice' — the Hebrew is literally 'speak peace (šālôm) but evil (rā'āh) is in their heart.' The very word for peace is weaponised. Language becomes a tool of betrayal.

hist.context: A lament that pivots to praise. David cries out (vv.1–2), asks for separation from the wicked (vv.3–4), and declares that God will repay the wicked for their deeds (v.5).

sections.header: Verses 6–9 — “The LORD Is My Strength and My Shield”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD is my strength and my shield (māgēn); my heart trusts in him, and he helps me" (v.7). The pivot: God heard, God helped, David’s heart leaps with joy. The personal becomes national: "save your people and bless your inheritance; be their shepherd and carry them forever" (v.9).

refs.note: "He tends his flock like a shepherd; he gathers the lambs in his arms" — the shepherd-carrying image.

notes.note: "The LORD is my strength and my shield; my heart trusts in him." Strength (offensive), shield (defensive), trust (relational). The full package.
"Be their shepherd and carry them forever." The psalm ends with the shepherd image from Psalm 23 applied to the entire nation.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the mood pivots sharply at v.6. 'Praise be' replaces 'hear my cry.' The tense shifts from petition to testimony. Alter notes the psalmist's confidence in answered prayer — possibly within the liturgy itself.

Alter: 'be their shepherd and carry them forever' — the psalm ends with the shepherd image, expanding from individual rescue (v.7) to communal care. The final verb (naśśā', 'carry') implies lifting a child.
Calvin: 'my heart trusts in him, and he helps me' — Calvin emphasises the order: trust comes first, then help. Faith is not a response to deliverance but the condition for experiencing it.

Calvin: the psalm concludes with intercession for 'your people' and 'your inheritance.' Calvin: private gratitude naturally overflows into public prayer. The individual never prays in isolation from the covenant community.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'his anointed one' (mešîḥô) — this royal reference places the psalm in the mouth of or on behalf of the king. The king is Yahweh's anointed representative, mediating divine strength to the people.
NET: 'your inheritance' (naḥalātekā) — Israel is God's inheritance, not merely the other way around. The possessive is reversed: God inherits a people. The language conveys intimacy and value.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the breakthrough from plea to praise happens between v.5 and v.6. No intervening narrative explains it — the psalm simply turns. Kidner suggests the turning itself is an act of faith rather than a response to visible change.
Kidner: 'shepherd and carry' — the shepherd carries the weak lambs. The final image is tenderness after the earlier martial language (shield, strength). God's power serves his gentleness.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the transition from lament to praise is a hallmark of Israel's prayer theology. Certainty of hearing precedes visible evidence of answered prayer. Faith generates the praise before the deliverance arrives.

VanGemeren: 'his anointed one' links the psalm to the Davidic covenant. The king's prayer for the people reflects the messianic ideal — the anointed ruler who intercedes for the nation. Christological overtones are present.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: 'my heart leaps for joy, and with my song I praise him' — the body responds: the heart leaps (physical), the voice sings (audible). Worship is not mental assent but embodied response.
Goldingay: the final verse broadens from 'I' to 'your people.' Goldingay notes this is characteristic of royal psalms — the king's personal prayer carries national significance.

hist.context: Thanksgiving erupts: "Praise be to the LORD, for he has heard my cry" (v.6). David’s heart trusts, and he is helped (v.7). The psalm expands: save your people, bless your inheritance, shepherd and carry them forever (v.9).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVRepay them for their deeds and for their evil work; repay them for what their hands have done and bring back on them what they deserve.ESVGive to them according to their work and according to the evil of their deeds; give to them according to the work of their hands; render them their due reward.NIVHear my cry for mercy as I call to you for help, as I lift up my hands toward your Most Holy Place.ESVHear the voice of my pleas for mercy, when I cry to you for help, when I lift up my hands toward your most holy sanctuary.

rec.text: ""To you I call, my rock. Hear my cry for mercy as I lift my hands toward your Most Holy Place." Prayer directed physically toward the temple — embodied worship."

rows.label: The Cry — 28:1–5
rows.text: "My rock, do not be deaf." Hands lifted. Separation from the wicked.

hebtext.note: "To you, LORD, I call; you are my rock (ṣūrî), do not turn a deaf ear to me" (v.1). David cries to God as rock — solid, immovable, reliable. Without God’s response, David is like those going down to the pit (v.1). He lifts his hands toward God’s inner sanctuary (v.2) and asks not to be dragged away with the wicked (vv.3–4).

"The LORD is my strength and my shield (māgēn); my heart trusts in him, and he helps me" (v.7). The pivot: God heard, God helped, David’s heart leaps with joy. The personal becomes national: "save your people and bless your inheritance; be their shepherd and carry them forever" (v.9).

thread.text: "The LORD is my rock" — the same ṣūr imagery.
"He tends his flock like a shepherd; he gathers the lambs in his arms" — the shepherd-carrying image.

themes.note: Faith: trust produces help. Sovereignty: God is rock, strength, shield. From personal cry to national prayer.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on 'The LORD is my strength and my shield,' consider asking God whether you lean on His strength or exhaust your own first and call on Him as a last resort.
